exemplar.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: Is our most revered knowledge more fragile than we assume it to be? Discuss with reference to the arts and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a clear and sustained focus on the prescribed title with effective definitions of key terms.
Employs relevant examples from both the arts and history to support central claims.
Acknowledges alternative viewpoints, showing some awareness of different perspectives.
Demonstrates coherent structure with a strong introduction and conclusion that reinforce the thesis.
weaknesses: Analysis often remains largely descriptive and does not systematically engage with TOK concepts (ways of knowing, methodology, justification).
Evaluation of counter-claims is brief and under-developed, limiting critical depth.
An explicit knowledge question is never articulated, weakening the TOK framing.
Signposting between comparisons of the arts and history is inconsistent, and some paragraphs end abruptly without full development.
Citation formatting is inconsistent, and some examples lack concrete details (dates, specific scholars).

annotations.comment: The Churchill discussion demonstrates awareness of conflicting perspectives, showing some evaluation of heroism versus colonial critique.
This discussion refers abstractly to historiansâ perspectives but lacks concrete examples of revisionist scholars or works; include specific cases to substantiate the point.
This analysis would benefit from explicit discussion of the ways of knowingâsuch as emotion and perceptionâto show how personal responses shape artistic knowledge.
The extension of the Shakespeare example to its continued staging illustrates the dynamic nature of interpretation and supports the claim of fragility.
The discussion of the arts lacks reference to specific methods of interpretation or ways of knowing; consider incorporating methodological analysis to deepen the argument.
While the Shakespeare example is relevant, the analysis remains largely descriptive; the student should probe the underlying epistemic mechanismsâsuch as how language shapes interpretation.
The Van Gogh example effectively demonstrates how shifts in taste and emotional response can transform the status of revered artistic knowledge.
The student establishes a clear, focused thesis and defines ârevered knowledgeâ and âfragilityâ effectively, providing a solid conceptual foundation for the discussion.
An explicit knowledge question is missing; the student should reframe the prompt into a clear TOK question (e.g., âTo what extent is revered knowledge fragile?â) to guide the analysis.
This paragraph offers insightful claims about cultural values but does not link them back to TOK concepts like perspective or justification; explicit connections would strengthen the analysis.
The discussion of Royâs novel ends abruptly and lacks completion; the student should finish the thought and explain how postcolonial readings reshape our knowledge.
The postcolonial lens is assumed rather than explained; the student should define this perspective and show how it systematically alters interpretation.
The citation attributes the Connor Beard quote incorrectly to (Johnson); accurate referencing is essential to maintain credibility and traceability.
The student asserts that new evidence reshapes history but does not discuss the criteria used to evaluate evidence; an analysis of evidence quality would deepen the argument.
Specific details or dates surrounding the Bengal Famine would strengthen the historical example and provide concrete evidence for the critique.
The analysis of Allied narratives in Nazi Germany shows critical awareness of victorsâ bias and the provisional nature of historical âtruth.â
While bias is mentioned, the student does not systematically apply TOK concepts like justification or methodology in history; integrate these to clarify how narratives are constructed.
The essay would benefit from clearer signposting of comparisons between the Arts and History to maintain a coherent structure throughout.
The concluding observations on fragility reiterate earlier points but do not explicitly consider the reliability of knowledge; draw out implications for knowledge validation.
The essay never articulates an explicit knowledge question in either the introduction or conclusion; presenting one would sharpen the TOK focus.
Ways of knowing such as memory and language are central to historical interpretation yet are not addressed; integrating these concepts would enrich the TOK analysis.
The conclusion effectively returns to the central claim and emphasizes the adaptive value of knowledge, reinforcing the essayâs coherence.
The final section relies on broad restatements without introducing new insight; consider incorporating a novel counterexample or implication.
Terms like âshared knowledgeâ and âpersonal knowledgeâ are central to TOK but are absent; integrating such terminology would demonstrate deeper conceptual understanding.
References in the Works Cited list show inconsistent formatting of titles and italics; apply a consistent citation style throughout.
Some entries lack full publication details or access dates; ensure each citation includes complete information for verification.

rubricCriterionStrandBandContents.content:
The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
